text:
CT CHEST / ABDOMEN / PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

EXAMINATION: Computed tomography of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with intravenous contrast (portal venous phase)

CLINICAL INDICATION: 63-year-old female with known stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma, status post right radical cytoreductive nephrectomy, currently on nivolumab maintenance therapy following four cycles of ipilimumab/nivolumab induction. Prior restaging CT approximately three months ago demonstrated partial response in pulmonary disease with a new indeterminate right pelvic sidewall lymph node. Restaging examination to assess treatment response and disease status.

COMPARISON: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast performed approximately three months prior.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of intravenous iodinated contrast material. Axial images were obtained with multiplanar reformations in the coronal and sagittal planes. Images reviewed on PACS workstation with standard lung, soft tissue, and bone windows.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are well-aerated bilaterally. There has been marked interval increase in the size of the dominant right lower lobe pulmonary nodule, which now measures 2.4 cm in its greatest dimension (previously 0.9 cm on the prior restaging CT approximately three months ago and 1.7 cm at the post-nephrectomy baseline). The nodule is round, solid, and smoothly marginated, situated in the posterior basilar segment, without associated pleural tethering or satellite nodularity in the immediate vicinity. A right upper lobe nodule in the posterior segment has grown substantially from 0.5 cm to 1.2 cm. In the left lower lobe, a posterior basilar nodule previously measured at 0.7 cm has increased to 1.5 cm; this lesion was the smallest responding lesion on prior imaging and now appears substantially more conspicuous with a slightly lobulated contour. The previously noted left upper lobe nodule, which had decreased to 0.3 cm at prior restaging, is again more clearly visible and measures approximately 0.5 cm, within the range of measurement variability but notable in the context of the broader trend of growth. Additionally, there are multiple new small bilateral pulmonary nodules scattered throughout both lungs, predominantly in the lower lobes, the largest of these measuring up to 0.4 cm in the right lower lobe; these were not identified on prior examination and raise concern for progressive hematogenous dissemination. No nodule demonstrates cavitation, surrounding ground-glass opacity, or features to suggest an infectious or inflammatory etiology. The central airways are patent. No endobronchial lesion is identified.

Pleurae: No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax. No pleural nodularity.

Mediastinum and Hila: The mediastinum is midline. Cardiac size is within normal limits for technique. No pericardial effusion. Aorta and central pulmonary arteries are unremarkable. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy is identified. The esophagus is unremarkable in caliber and course.

Chest Wall and Bony Thorax: No acute osseous abnormality identified in the visualized ribs, sternum, clavicles, or thoracic vertebral bodies. Mild degenerative changes are noted at the lower thoracic spine. The musculature and subcutaneous tissues of the chest wall are unremarkable.

---

ABDOMEN:

Nephrectomy Bed: The right nephrectomy bed is well-defined. Postoperative changes are redemonstrated with surgical clips in the right renal fossa and along the course of the resected renal vein. There is no focal mass, fluid collection, or hematoma in the right nephrectomy bed to suggest local recurrence. The right adrenal gland is present and normal in morphology, without nodularity or enlargement.

Contralateral Kidney and Urinary Tract: The left kidney is of normal size and configuration, demonstrating symmetric cortical enhancement without focal parenchymal abnormality. No hydronephrosis, nephrolithiasis, or perirenal abnormality. The left ureter is not dilated. The urinary bladder is adequately distended and smooth-walled.

Retroperitoneum and Lymph Nodes: The retroperitoneal lymph node dissection bed is again identified. Surgical clips are present in the right para-aortic and aortocaval regions. There is no discrete soft tissue nodularity or convincing lymphadenopathy in the retroperitoneum above the aortic bifurcation to suggest nodal recurrence in the dissected field. The abdominal aorta and inferior vena cava are patent.

Liver: The liver is normal in size, with smooth hepatic contours and homogeneous parenchymal enhancement. No focal hepatic lesion is identified. The intrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated.

Biliary System: The gallbladder is present and contains no stones or wall thickening. The extrahepatic bile duct is of normal caliber.

Pancreas: The pancreas enhances homogeneously and is normal in size and morphology throughout the head, body, and tail. No pancreatic ductal dilation. No peripancreatic abnormality.

Spleen: The spleen is normal in size and demonstrates homogeneous enhancement. No splenic lesion.

Adrenal Glands: Left adrenal gland is unremarkable. Right adrenal gland is present and normal (as above).

Bowel: The small bowel loops are normal in caliber and wall thickness. The colon demonstrates no mural thickening or dilation. No free intraperitoneal air. No ascites.

---

PELVIS:

Lymph Nodes: There has been substantial interval growth in the right pelvic sidewall lymph node, previously noted as indeterminate at 0.8 cm on the prior CT. It now measures 1.9 cm in short-axis dimension, with rounded morphology and internal heterogeneity, markedly more concerning for active nodal metastasis in the context of the patient's known metastatic RCC. Additionally, a new right external iliac lymph node measuring 1.4 cm in short axis has appeared since the prior examination. The left iliac and obturator nodal chains appear unremarkable. No bilateral inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Organs: The uterus is normal in size and morphology for the patient's age. No adnexal mass or complex ovarian lesion. Bladder is as described above.

Bony Pelvis: No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesion suspicious for metastatic involvement. Mild bilateral sacroiliac joint degenerative changes.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Marked interval growth of all previously identified bilateral pulmonary metastases compared to the restaging CT obtained approximately three months ago, with the dominant right lower lobe nodule growing from 0.9 cm to 2.4 cm and the left lower lobe nodule growing from 0.7 cm to 1.5 cm. The degree of interval enlargement across all pulmonary sites is substantial and clearly exceeds measurement variability.

2. Multiple new small bilateral pulmonary nodules up to 0.4 cm, most conspicuous in the lower lobes, not present on prior examination, consistent with progressive hematogenous pulmonary dissemination.

3. Marked interval growth of the right pelvic sidewall lymph node (0.8 cm to 1.9 cm, short axis), previously indeterminate and now morphologically consistent with active metastatic nodal involvement.

4. New right external iliac lymph node measuring 1.4 cm (short axis), consistent with new nodal metastasis.

5. No hepatic, adrenal, or osseous metastatic lesions identified.

6. No local recurrence in the right nephrectomy bed or previously dissected retroperitoneal nodal field.

Overall, the findings are most consistent with significant multifocal disease progression involving bilateral pulmonary parenchyma and right pelvic/iliac nodal stations, occurring in the setting of nivolumab maintenance therapy following an initial partial response to combination ipilimumab/nivolumab induction. The overall disease burden has increased substantially compared to the prior examination. Clinical correlation and reassessment of systemic therapy are warranted.
